questions.explanation: The <a> (anchor) element creates hyperlinks using the href attribute.
questions.question: Which HTML element is used to create a hyperlink to another page or resource?

questions.explanation: Padding adds space between the content and the element’s border; margin is outside the border.
questions.question: Which CSS property is primarily used to control the spacing inside an element’s border?

questions.explanation: let is block-scoped and allows reassignment; const is block-scoped but cannot be reassigned.
questions.question: Which JavaScript keyword declares a block-scoped variable that can be reassigned?

questions.explanation: GET is intended for safe, read-only retrieval of resources.
questions.question: In HTTP, which method is typically used to retrieve data without changing server state?

questions.explanation: 201 Created is the standard response when a server successfully creates a new resource.
questions.question: In a REST API, which HTTP status code best indicates that a new resource was successfully created?

questions.explanation: TypeScript adds static typing, enabling compile-time checks that reduce runtime type errors.
questions.question: Which TypeScript feature helps catch errors by enforcing types at compile time?

questions.explanation: The dependency array determines when the effect re-runs: on mount and whenever any dependency changes.

options.A: To define initial state values for the component
options.B: To memoize the component so it never re-renders
options.C: To control when the effect runs based on changes to listed values
options.D: To automatically bind 'this' in event handlers

questions.question: In React, what is the primary purpose of the dependency array in useEffect?

questions.explanation: JWT access tokens are commonly sent on each request in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header.

options.B: In a custom HTML meta tag
options.C: In the Authorization header using the Bearer scheme
options.D: In the response body of the previous request only

questions.question: You are building an Express (Node.js) API that uses JWTs. Where should the server typically look for the access token on each request?

questions.explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps all users and matches orders when present; COUNT(o.id) yields 0 for users with no orders.

options.A: SELECT u.email, COUNT(o.id) FROM users u INNER JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id GROUP BY u.email;
options.B: SELECT u.email, COUNT(o.id) FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id GROUP BY u.email;
options.C: SELECT email, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY email;
options.D: SELECT u.email, SUM(o.id) FROM users u RIGHT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id GROUP BY u.email;

questions.question: Given a SQL table users(id, email) and orders(id, user_id, total), which query returns each user’s email and the number of orders they have (including users with zero orders)?

questions.explanation: Using a union type (string | null) forces callers and the function body to account for null explicitly under strictNullChecks.
questions.question: You are migrating a JavaScript codebase to TypeScript. A function accepts either a string or null. Which TypeScript signature best enforces handling of null under strictNullChecks?
